2605.10216.title: The Impact of Editorial Intervention on Detecting Native Language Traces
2605.10216.paper_summary: Native Language Identification (NLI) is the task of determining an author's native language (L1) from their non-native writings. With the advent of human-AI co-authorship, non-native texts are routinely corrected and rewritten by large language models, fundamentally altering the linguistic features NLI models depend on. In this paper, we investigate the robustness of L1 traces across increasing degrees of editorial intervention. By processing 450 essays from the Write & Improve 2024 corpus through varying levels of grammatical error correction (GEC) and paraphrasing, we demonstrate that L1 attribution does not entirely depend on surface-level errors. Instead, the detection models leverage deeper L1 features: unidiomatic lexico-semantic choices, pragmatic transfer, and the author's underlying cultural perspective. We find that minimal edits preserve these structural traces and maintain high profiling accuracy. In contrast, fluency edits and paraphrasing normalize these L1 features, leading to a severe degradation in performance.
2605.10216.paper_summary_zh: 母語識別（NLI）是從作者的非母語寫作中確定其母語（L1）的任務。隨著人類與人工智慧共同創作的興起，非母語文本經常被大型語言模型進行修正和重寫，這從根本上改變了NLI模型所依賴的語言特徵。在本文中，我們研究了在不同程度的編輯干預下L1痕跡的穩健性。通過對2024年Write & Improve語料庫中的450篇文章進行不同程度的語法錯誤修正（GEC）和改寫，我們展示了L1歸屬並不完全依賴於表面層次的錯誤。相反，檢測模型利用了更深層的L1特徵：不地道的詞彙語義選擇、語用轉移以及作者潛在的文化視角。我們發現，最小的編輯保留了這些結構痕跡並維持了高準確度的特徵分析。相比之下，流暢性編輯和改寫則使這些L1特徵正常化，導致性能的嚴重下降。
2605.10216.author: Ahmet Yavuz Uluslu et.al.
2605.10216.authors: Ahmet Yavuz Uluslu, Mark Gales, Kate Knill, Gerold Schneider
